relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Job begins his final oath with a covenant over his eyes.
He asks what portion would come from Shaddai if he betrayed integrity.
He knows calamity belongs with injustice.
Every way and step is seen.
He denies walking with falsehood and deceit.
He asks to be weighed in honest scales.
If his heart followed his eyes into stain, let consequence come.
If he sowed falsely, let another eat.
He denies hidden betrayal of a neighbor’s household.
He invokes consequence if that betrayal were true.
Such an act would be shameful and judged.
It would be a consuming fire to Abaddon.
He denies rejecting the case of servants who contended with him.
He asks how he could answer the Divine if he did.
The same Maker formed master and servant in the womb.
He denies withholding desire from the poor or failing the widow’s eyes.
He denies eating alone while the orphan went hungry.
He remembers guiding the vulnerable from youth.
He denies seeing the needy uncovered and doing nothing.
The warmed body of the poor blessed him.
He denies using gate-power against the orphan.
If so, let the arm of power be broken.
Awe before divine majesty restrained him from such harm.
He denies making gold his confidence.
He denies worshiping the power of his own wealth.
He denies being seduced by sun or moon.
He denies secret heart-idolatry.
That too would deny the Divine above.
He denies rejoicing at an enemy’s ruin.
He did not let his mouth curse for another’s life.
His tent was known for feeding others.
The sojourner found open doors.
He denies hiding guilt like Adam/humanity.
He denies being silenced by fear of the crowd.
He longs for a hearing, a written charge, and Shaddai’s answer.
He would wear the case openly like honor.
He would approach like a prince and account for his steps.
He calls the land itself as witness.
He denies exploiting land or owners without cost.
If false, let thorns replace wheat; Job’s words come to their end.
